6b3e40dc66392b2676d45f6a7427b7ac676054dc.tech: Notion iterated on an agent harness about 4–5 times (late 2022–2026) before shipping Custom Agents to production. Key shifts included moving from code-writing and custom XML to Notion-flavored markdown plus SQL-like queries over SQLite, then shifting from few-shot prompts to declarative tool definitions and progressive tool disclosure. Their evaluation stack runs regression tests in CI, launch-quality evals with 80–90% pass rates, and “Notion’s Last Exam” frontier/headroom evals designed to pass ~30%. The production system exposes 100+ tools, uses memory via pages/databases, and supports agent composition via manager agents and setup tools that generate system prompts, schemas, and permissions (with human confirmation for permission changes).
6b3e40dc66392b2676d45f6a7427b7ac676054dc.nontech: Notion spent multiple years rebuilding its agent system before releasing Custom Agents for enterprise customers. The goal was to let users automate complex workflows across their workspaces while meeting enterprise expectations for reliability, security, and cost control. The final production setup supports a large library of tools and powers workflows like bug triaging, email processing, and meeting notes capture.

fce49d51125a51b5bd26c1661e14e4da119ef62b.tech: Duolingo built an AI-powered Slack agent to standardize access to Model Context Protocol (MCP) infrastructure. After MCP setup proved too complex, they centralized discovery, then standardized MCP servers with HTTP endpoints and JWT-in-header patterns. The Slack app uses the Claude Agent SDK plus Slack Bot SDK, supports read-only tools and AWS CLI/BigQuery diagnostics, and uses human-in-the-loop confirmation with Temporal workflows for write operations.
fce49d51125a51b5bd26c1661e14e4da119ef62b.nontech: Duolingo created an AI Slack bot to make its Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools easier for employees to use. Instead of asking people to set up MCP servers themselves, the bot brings help desk and incident support into Slack, with safe approvals for actions that change things. By April 2026, it reached over 250 weekly users and reduced on-call toil.

594751499b39ec05a77112236d1184cc8bccd9f5.tech: DoorDash uses a hybrid LLM + deep learning architecture for hyper-personalized merchandising. LLMs handle product understanding, natural-language consumer profile generation, and weekly content blueprint creation, grounded with RAG over ontology/taxonomy/catalog. Offline processing pre-generates carousels; online uses embeddings-based retrieval (10–20 per carousel) and final ranking via two-tower embeddings and MTML rankers. GEPA optimization in DSPy tunes prompts/templates, search-term logic, profile scope, and ranking objectives, evaluated with quantitative metrics, LLM-as-judge, and human feedback. Tools include LangChain, LlamaIndex, vector DBs (Pinecone/ChromaDB/Qdrant), FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch, and microservices/orchestration.
594751499b39ec05a77112236d1184cc8bccd9f5.nontech: DoorDash built a personalization system to help shoppers find relevant items across a large, changing local commerce catalog. It combines LLMs for understanding products and creating personalized merchandising plans with traditional models for fast, accurate ranking. The system adapts to real-time intent while managing latency and cost, and it improves quality using quantitative results, LLM judging, and human feedback.

09d048c7554ea9fb4bfcd47e9a220136ce25f11e.tech: Paperclip is an open-source agent orchestration platform for production multi-agent business automation, using a centralized control plane with organizational hierarchies, task management, quality assurance workflows, and vendor-neutral agent integration. It supports multiple model providers (including Claude and GPT models via Codex, plus Gemini, Pi, Hermes) through OpenRouter, with agent memory/context managed inside the system.
09d048c7554ea9fb4bfcd47e9a220136ce25f11e.nontech: Paperclip is an open-source platform that helps coordinate multiple AI agents across different business functions. It provides a centralized way to assign work, manage quality checks, and integrate agents from different vendors. The creator used it to run their own development and marketing video production, and it gained rapid community interest after release.

a0b1743bff4e6e04b8cb38559d5ea0f9e1f34d95.tech: OpsWorker.ai’s multi-agent AI SRE system targets Kubernetes-based microservices incidents where rule-based alerting can’t handle complex, data-heavy failure chains. Eight specialized agents collaborate: an Orchestrator coordinates; others handle topology mapping, signals correlation, change analysis, root-cause reasoning, remediation planning, prevention recommendations, and policy enforcement. The workflow correlates logs, metrics, and traces into timelines and dependency graphs, producing auditable, structured outputs and Slack-ready summaries.
a0b1743bff4e6e04b8cb38559d5ea0f9e1f34d95.nontech: OpsWorker.ai built a multi-agent AI system for incident response and root-cause analysis in cloud-native, Kubernetes-based environments. Instead of relying only on alerts and simple rules, it runs a structured investigation that connects system signals, changes, and dependencies. The goal is faster resolution and better learning from incidents, with safety controls and audit trails.

d2e976215d329e4c35fae2ae7441c1fe195e53c0.tech: LinkedIn’s Cognitive Memory Agent (CMA) is a horizontal memory platform for stateful, context-aware AI agents. It uses multi-layer memory (conversational, episodic, semantic, procedural) with embeddings stored in vector databases, plus streaming and batch pipelines for summarization, extraction, and aggregation. Retrieval is implemented as an orchestrated reasoning loop that plans memory tool calls across layers before answer synthesis, with an open-source LLM on LinkedIn internal infrastructure. Access control isolates memory per application and per tenant, with ownership metadata and session-limited access. A three-tier evaluation framework measures memory representation quality, response quality via LLM-as-judge, and end-to-end product performance via offline and online tests, while tracking latency and cost constraints.
d2e976215d329e4c35fae2ae7441c1fe195e53c0.nontech: LinkedIn built the Cognitive Memory Agent (CMA) to help AI agents stay useful over time, not just within a single conversation. CMA stores and retrieves relevant information across multiple memory types, so agents can maintain continuity beyond context limits and improve through personalized memory. It was integrated into Hiring Assistant to reduce recruiter friction by suggesting roles from past projects, auto-filling hiring requirements, and surfacing insights from historical activity.

173641090125a89cbab1b1e6dcac196e7ae3b785.tech: LinkedIn built an agentic platform that treats AI agents as a first-class execution model alongside microservices infrastructure. It separates “foreground agents” (IDE-integrated tools) from “background agents” (autonomous task executors) running in secure sandboxes. Agents use MCP for standardized tool calling, generate pull requests, and cannot directly merge code—changes go through standard review, testing, and ownership checks. The platform includes RAG with a semantic index, PR semantic descriptions, and evals using quantitative checks (builds/tests/static analysis) plus qualitative PR-history-based assessment. Observability audits every step/tool call/decision with replayable execution traces and identity-based permissions.
173641090125a89cbab1b1e6dcac196e7ae3b785.nontech: LinkedIn needed to move AI agents from isolated experiments into production systems that could support thousands of developers. It created a unified platform where agents help with real engineering work by generating pull requests for normal review, rather than making changes on their own. The system uses secure execution, standardized tool access, strong context retrieval, and built-in evaluation and monitoring to maintain quality, compliance, and visibility at enterprise scale.

587ff5a5e8e703b9384ba90e02810d0712c69a9e.tech: Uber scaled AI capabilities by building an internal “Agentic Marketplace” for Claude skills, starting with two skills (CI log triage/repair and a basic code reviewer) and growing to 200+ curated skills in the main hub plus 300+ experimental tools in team repositories. It used two-tier governance: a “Golden Marketplace” with manual code review, CI/CD checks, and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, plus a sandbox tier shared via URLs and manually loaded. Production skills required deterministic outputs, exact diffs, and reporting of attempted/succeeded/failed actions, with engineers retaining final review authority. The approach emphasized prompt engineering, agent-based/multi-agent systems, human-in-the-loop, evals, error handling, and reliability/scalability via CI/CD and orchestration.
587ff5a5e8e703b9384ba90e02810d0712c69a9e.nontech: Uber faced the challenge of scaling AI use across a large engineering organization with 200+ microservices and thousands of engineers. Instead of a top-down mandate, a grassroots effort created an internal marketplace for Claude AI skills. It grew from two skills in October 2024 to 500+ specialized skills in five months, driven by engineer demand. A two-level system kept mission-critical tools tightly controlled while allowing fast experimentation, leading to broad adoption, automated code review and verification workflows, and wider access to senior engineering knowledge.

7dbfd27f86007fb0e390aa45b978d485ba366b3e.tech: Komodor’s Klaudia AI uses a three-layer multi-agent architecture for cloud-native incident management. It coordinates 50+ domain SME agents (e.g., Kubernetes, GPU/NVIDIA, AWS, ArgoCD, Istio) via workflow orchestrators running Detect → Investigate → Remediate → Optimize → Prevent. A knowledge graph maps entity relationships for relationship-aware retrieval, alongside a RAG-style Knowledge Base Query Agent using vector databases.
7dbfd27f86007fb0e390aa45b978d485ba366b3e.nontech: Komodor introduced Klaudia AI to help manage incidents in complex cloud environments where symptoms and root causes can be far apart. It uses many specialized AI agents coordinated by workflows, plus a knowledge graph and retrieval from documentation and past incidents. Komodor reports improvements like reduced MTTR for Kubernetes issues and faster pipeline failure diagnosis.

37ea752eafb0abdf850f49ab0ad42957bc85c9ea.tech: Madrigal built an enterprise multi-agent research platform for pharmaceutical data integration using LangChain’s DeepAgents and LangSmith. Specialized agents for search, analysis, and synthesis run in parallel under an orchestrator, with data normalized into a consistent tool interface and stored in a secure data warehouse. A virtual filesystem provides shared collaborative memory. LangSmith tracing and trace-level evals support debugging and continuous improvement, while LangSmith Deploy provides managed deployment with state persistence, concurrent sessions, and streaming via CI/CD redeployments.
37ea752eafb0abdf850f49ab0ad42957bc85c9ea.nontech: Madrigal Pharmaceuticals created an enterprise platform to integrate, search, and synthesize information from different pharmaceutical data sources, including structured systems, documents, and external sources. The system uses role-based permissions and governance guardrails so users access only authorized information, with responses clearly cited. It helped reduce time to build new use cases and speed up production deployment, while enabling domain experts to contribute to agent skills.

167f12420e42c21e1280a43ec3e7c11b1deb1dd2.tech: Notion deployed Custom Agents across the organization, using a RAG-style setup where agents search Notion databases, wikis, documentation, and knowledge bases for context. Agents integrate with Slack and other systems, run on event-driven triggers and scheduled executions, and support domain-specific workflows like question-answering and automated classification/routing.
167f12420e42c21e1280a43ec3e7c11b1deb1dd2.nontech: Notion rolled out AI agents across teams to automate repetitive work and reduce manual busywork. The company addressed knowledge access issues, manual triage of product feedback, and time-consuming repetitive tasks by deploying domain-specific agents that connect to tools teams already use. Reported outcomes include faster customer support responses, automatic task creation/routing, and broad adoption across departments.

900dff699cf4532c3844a176771a838d90063abb.tech: ElevenLabs describes a cascaded voice pipeline: speech-to-text transcription, language models for translation/reasoning, and text-to-speech to regenerate audio in the target language while preserving characteristics. They add emotion detection in transcription and pass emotional state to the language model to guide tone. They also discuss fused omni-models, highlighting tradeoffs in latency versus reliability and guardrails.
900dff699cf4532c3844a176771a838d90063abb.nontech: ElevenLabs built production voice AI for tasks like transcription, translation, and voice generation at scale. They use a multi-step approach that combines understanding speech, translating with reasoning, and generating voice output with the right tone. The company also invested in deployment infrastructure for enterprise customers, aiming for quality, reliability, and expressive voice results.

083687a3fb8ea5d65faa5857647a8c7dca08e3ec.tech: ListenLabs runs a multi-agent platform for user research at scale, with three primary agents: Composer for artifact-based discussion guide creation, a voice-based multimodal interview agent, and a research agent that analyzes qualitative data. The research agent uses a conceptual virtual table over PostgreSQL, parallelized sub-agent execution, custom reviewer/evaluation agents, RAG/semantic search with embeddings, sandboxed Python via E2B, and trace-based observability.
083687a3fb8ea5d65faa5857647a8c7dca08e3ec.nontech: ListenLabs helps teams analyze user research at scale by turning interviews, surveys, and focus group feedback into automated insights and deliverables. The platform supports the full workflow from study design through data collection to reporting. It uses multiple specialized AI agents, quality checks, and incremental updates so users can see early results and receive polished outputs like charts and PowerPoint presentations.

29b847fdfb6e2a8d2923c0c3d610c66cb24caf2e.tech: Spotify built Ads AI, a multi-agent system for media planning, using Google Agent Development Kit (ADK) v0.2.0 and Vertex AI Gemini 2.5 Pro. A RouterAgent conditionally triggers specialized agents (Goal, Audience, Budget, Schedule) in parallel. Agents call tool-backed function tools, use PostgreSQL plus in-memory caching, and run on Spotify’s Apollo framework with gRPC and structured outputs.
29b847fdfb6e2a8d2923c0c3d610c66cb24caf2e.nontech: Spotify’s advertising planning had fragmented decision logic across Direct, Self-Serve, and Programmatic channels, creating duplicated work and tech debt. To unify planning, it built Ads AI, a multi-agent system that turns natural-language campaign needs into optimized media plans. Media plan creation dropped from 15–30 minutes to 5–10 seconds, using historical results from thousands of campaigns.

bcf1ac76dfddc906da19e40251d0ca3b35a4e178.tech: GitHub Copilot CLI with Claude Opus 4.6 and VSCode powers an “agent-first development” workflow for building eval-agents. The Copilot SDK provides access to tools and Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Development uses /plan then /autopilot, followed by a Copilot Code Review loop, with CI/CD guardrails like strict typing, linters, and integration/end-to-end/contract tests.
bcf1ac76dfddc906da19e40251d0ca3b35a4e178.nontech: A GitHub Copilot Applied Science team built a tool called eval-agents to automate analysis of large agent-trajectory logs from evaluation benchmarks like TerminalBench2 and SWEBench-Pro. Using an agent-first approach plus documentation, testing, and CI/CD guardrails, five researchers onboarded quickly and delivered many new agents and skills in under three days, with thousands of code changes across hundreds of files.

04f321b29895b5fe9d5201fe5e56b1b96a53edf0.tech: Datadog reports operating over 100 AI agents in production and preparing to scale to thousands more. Deployed agents include Bits AI SRE for autonomous alert investigation, Bits AI Dev for code generation and error fixes, and security analysts for automated security investigations. Core practices include API-first, agent-first design via an MCP server, proactive background agents orchestrated with Temporal, containerized sandboxes, and three-layer evaluation (offline, online, and adaptive).
04f321b29895b5fe9d5201fe5e56b1b96a53edf0.nontech: Datadog shares lessons from running more than 100 AI agents in production and planning to scale to thousands more. It describes agents that handle alert investigation, propose code fixes, and automate security checklists. The main takeaway is that success at scale depends less on “intelligence” and more on operational excellence, strong monitoring, and solid LLMOps practices.

76cefbf1326cb3d1bbc524c0d8f3ee3bf8e9f102.tech: Coinbase’s Enterprise Applications and Architecture team ran a six-week “Agentic AI Tiger Team” to standardize enterprise AI agents for internal process automation. They adopted a code-first approach using LangGraph and LangChain (instead of low-code) to improve reproducibility, testability, and auditability. The architecture separates deterministic data nodes from probabilistic LLM nodes, with observability-first tracing, evaluation harnesses, LLM-as-judge spot checks, and human-in-the-loop approvals.
76cefbf1326cb3d1bbc524c0d8f3ee3bf8e9f102.nontech: Coinbase’s Enterprise Applications and Architecture team formed an “Agentic AI Tiger Team” over six weeks to standardize how enterprise AI agents are built and deployed for internal work. They chose a code-first approach to make agent behavior easier to test and audit, which is important for regulatory compliance in finance. They deployed two production automations saving 25+ hours per week and reduced future agent development time from quarters to days.

f578fe0a029cd08146fdf12d743641f6d0824fb0.tech: Atlassian evolved Rovo Chat from a single-agent RAG baseline to hierarchical multi-agent orchestration. The hybrid orchestrator decomposes complex queries into subtasks via prompt engineering, routes to domain-specialized subagents (e.g., a Jira Agent using JQL documentation search, JQL execution, and entity linking), and uses parallel tool calling one step at a time. Dynamic reasoning modes include brainstorming (no tools), tool QnA (parallel tools), and multi-step reasoning (sequential dependent tools).
f578fe0a029cd08146fdf12d743641f6d0824fb0.nontech: Atlassian improved Rovo Chat, its enterprise conversational assistant for knowledge retrieval and workflow automation. They moved from a single-agent setup to a hierarchical multi-agent system because one agent struggled with many tools and domains while keeping quality and speed. The new approach breaks questions into smaller parts handled by specialized experts, improving quality and reducing response time.

5a215d05e134164256672da6959dfae6b350a79d.tech: Coinbase’s RAPID-D augments its RAPID decision framework with a four-agent multi-agent architecture: an Analyst reviews the RAPID document, a Seeker generates questions and performs retrieval-augmented generation from enterprise knowledge bases, a Devil’s Advocate constructs arguments against the initial recommendation, and a Synthesizer evaluates and synthesizes trade-offs. It uses Claude 3.7 Sonnet, an asynchronous architecture for complex decisions, and includes a key-question generator for RAG. Human-in-the-loop evaluation compares recommendations to actual RAPID Decider outcomes, and real-time stakeholder feedback is analyzed to optimize subsequent recommendations within the same decision flow. Tools include FastAPI, monitoring, and documentation, plus error handling and evals.
5a215d05e134164256672da6959dfae6b350a79d.nontech: Coinbase built RAPID-D, an internal AI decision support tool for critical strategic decisions in finance. It is designed to help people make better choices by surfacing unseen risks and reducing cognitive bias. The system uses a multi-agent approach to analyze proposals, pull relevant internal context, challenge assumptions, and produce a synthesized recommendation for human decision-makers. It also incorporates stakeholder comments during the same decision process to improve later recommendations, and it is evaluated through human review against actual decisions.

714b7a8b616ce46b516577890503c5799e8aed64.tech: OpenDev is an open-source, terminal-native AI coding agent written in Rust that uses a compound multi-model architecture with per-workflow LLM binding. It separates thinking and action (extended ReAct), applies Adaptive Context Compaction with graduated reduction strategies, and uses dual-memory (episodic summaries plus bounded working memory). Safety is defense-in-depth with schema-level tool gating, runtime approvals, and tool validation. It supports lazy tool discovery via MCP, LSP integration with caching, subagent orchestration, and terminal/web UIs (Textual and FastAPI/WebSockets).
714b7a8b616ce46b516577890503c5799e8aed64.nontech: OpenDev is an open-source command-line AI coding assistant designed to work in real developer workflows, including managing source control, running builds, and deploying environments. It focuses on three problems: keeping long conversations within limited context, reducing the risk of destructive actions, and expanding capabilities without overwhelming token limits. It uses multiple models, smarter context trimming, and layered safety to improve reliability over longer sessions.

fb338d9298703da9a557d7b56aefaabfac576570.tech: Notion deploys LLMs at scale using a multi-provider architecture to maintain optionality across model providers. Its “Auto” model feature evaluates major models on a cost-per-capability-per-second basis and switches every 2–3 weeks, handling ~75% of AI traffic. Architecture and orchestration (orchestration, compaction, caching, context management) account for up to 3× cost swings beyond model choice, with eval scorecards and feedback to providers.
fb338d9298703da9a557d7b56aefaabfac576570.nontech: Notion describes how it keeps LLM costs and performance competitive for millions of users despite volatile pricing, model deprecations, and supplier competition. It uses a multi-provider setup and an “Auto” system that automatically picks and switches models every 2–3 weeks. Notion also improves costs through infrastructure work and uses open-weight options for routine tasks, reporting up to 3× cost reductions and serving 75% of AI traffic via automated model selection.

3d2c2ce692d6129caadc5e212134c3a217b57438.tech: Cara used Claude Code (Opus 4.6) to autonomously execute 66 software tickets across 2 repositories, writing 536 tests and adding ~20,000 lines of code. They implemented RePPITS (Research, Propose, Plan, Implement, Test, Secure) with persistent file-based memory, parallel subagents, phase gates, and security audits. The output formed a composable 5-layer architecture (Foundation, Runtime, Capability, Adapter, Specialty) with a dependency resolver, policy-driven planner, and deterministic clinical safety engine.
3d2c2ce692d6129caadc5e212134c3a217b57438.nontech: Cara, a healthcare software platform company, used an LLM coding tool to complete 66 software tickets and deliver a composable architecture for its healthcare app platform in under 4 hours. The work required 20–25 hours of preparation. Built-in testing and security checks helped catch safety-critical issues, resulting in zero deferred issues and only one minor production incident resolved in under 5 minutes.

d3be9d07a755f87cf5bc9594c05bc8ddbc63c7af.tech: Teads integrated AI agents into its ML experiment orchestration platform “Datakinator” using MCP (Model Context Protocol). The MCP server exposed existing API routes, while the agent was enriched with context tools for dataset probing, error retrieval, feature metadata/documentation, and model configuration requirements. This enabled autonomous error correction (detect failure, retrieve errors, archive, correct configuration, relaunch) and included cost estimation with permission and refusal thresholds. The platform orchestrates hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, and model training at scale on cloud GPUs.
d3be9d07a755f87cf5bc9594c05bc8ddbc63c7af.nontech: Teads, a digital advertising technology company, updated its ML experiment platform “Datakinator” so data scientists could launch experiments with less tedious setup. By using AI agents through MCP, the system automated experiment configuration and orchestration, including checking datasets and retrieving errors. After release, more than 200 experiments launched in 48 hours, with claimed 5–10% uplift in offline metrics and about $1M in direct margin gains, while temporary cloud cost spikes were later managed with cost controls.

954ad6f60960c502da4eccc045a9476ab1bae5d6.tech: Whatnot built an internal enterprise LLM platform for production workflows across trust & safety, customer support, and seller assistance. The platform emphasizes velocity (self-serve prompt experimentation with guardrails and post-exposure logging), trust (LLM-as-judge evaluation with calibration workflows and rubrics), and reliability (multi-provider support, default fallbacks, observability, caching, rate limiting, and guardrails).
954ad6f60960c502da4eccc045a9476ab1bae5d6.nontech: Whatnot, a live shopping e-commerce platform, created an internal LLM platform to support trust & safety, customer support, and seller help. Instead of focusing only on calling LLM APIs, it built infrastructure to speed up prompt changes, improve confidence in outputs through evaluation, and keep services available. The platform helped trust reviewers process harassment reports in minutes rather than hours.

51e9b9b285cc31f9c25c61e6de793d3d2e506c47.tech: The case study compares three production LLM systems trained with reinforcement learning for agentic tasks: Kimi K2.5, Cursor Composer 2, and Chroma Context-1. All address context-window limits, training-to-production gaps, and reward functions that avoid degenerate behavior. Kimi uses Agent Swarm with PARL and outcome-based rewards plus generative reward models; Cursor uses real-time RL from production traffic with an asynchronous Ray/PyTorch stack; Chroma trains a self-editing search agent with a token-budgeted harness and extraction-verified synthetic data.
51e9b9b285cc31f9c25c61e6de793d3d2e506c47.nontech: The case study looks at how three tech teams trained AI agents to work reliably in real products. They all faced similar problems: keeping enough information during long sessions, making training match what happens in production, and designing rewards that don’t lead the agent to “cheat.” The results include faster, more accurate agent behavior for browsing, coding, and finding relevant documents.

16e45007e1f483686714e95459e4a7afd5046bc9.tech: Guidesly’s Jack AI is an AWS serverless, event-driven pipeline that turns uploaded trip media (photos, videos, metadata) into marketing-ready assets. It uses API Gateway to trigger AWS Step Functions, Lambda for stages (EXIF extraction, computer vision inference, media optimization, content generation, publishing), S3 for artifacts, RDS (PostgreSQL) for structured data, and Amazon Bedrock foundation models for generation with contextual prompting and constraints.
16e45007e1f483686714e95459e4a7afd5046bc9.nontech: Guidesly built Jack AI to help outdoor guides spend less time on marketing. After a trip, guides upload photos and videos, and the system automatically creates marketing content for websites, social media, and email. Guides saw content generation time drop from 13 minutes to 2 minutes and reported major revenue growth for active users.

95c331e5dab690499dd634ede4f0513e1de7dd89.tech: Hex evolved from single-shot text-to-SQL in notebook cells (GPT-3.5 Turbo) to multi-agent systems spanning entire projects. Agents include Notebook, Threads, Semantic Modeling, and Chat with App, unified via shared modular capability bundles. Hex built custom orchestration with long-running workflows on Temporal, plus context harvesting pipelines, tool search/retrieval, ephemeral SQL execution, and evaluation frameworks for long-horizon tasks.
95c331e5dab690499dd634ede4f0513e1de7dd89.nontech: Hex, a collaborative data analytics platform, moved from simple one-off question answering to multi-agent help for complex, iterative analysis. It built long-running workflows and project-level context so agents can produce comprehensive reports over time. The company also invested in evaluation and privacy-preserving monitoring to improve reliability, while still working on verification and handling conflicting context.

8dd7999c69b6b6a7a570f7812fc477e6d04c1dd6.tech: Langchain frames production AI agents as “model plus harness,” where the harness includes context engineering, prompting (system prompts, tool descriptions, skill front matter, sub-agent specs), verification hooks, and orchestration logic. It emphasizes continual learning via trace mining, context injection, and fine-tuning open models, plus evaluation flywheels using Langsmith and custom suites.
8dd7999c69b6b6a7a570f7812fc477e6d04c1dd6.nontech: Langchain’s approach to building production AI agents focuses on wrapping the model with the right “harness” around it—clear instructions, built-in checks, and orchestration logic—so agents can reliably complete specific tasks. They stress custom evaluations tied to real customer needs and continual improvement from production traces, rather than relying only on frontier model power.

8c82e95ab2beff01c476ad7e93105eef50724bbb.tech: Vercel built and operates production AI agents using its agent orchestration platform. The d0 internal analytics agent is a text-to-SQL engine that uses Vercel Sandboxes for isolated execution, Fluid Compute for dynamic scaling, AI Gateway for multi-model routing, and Workflows for durable orchestration with retries and state recovery. Built-in observability traces prompts, model responses, and decision paths. Vercel’s AI SDK provides streaming responses, tool use, and structured outputs, including a Chatbot primitive for Slack delivery.
8c82e95ab2beff01c476ad7e93105eef50724bbb.nontech: Vercel says building AI agents is easier now, but running them reliably at scale is still hard. It describes d0, an internal analytics agent that answers hundreds of data questions daily. Vercel attributes fast development to its platform primitives, and says the same infrastructure supports multiple internal agents and customer-facing products like v0 and Vercel Agent for PR reviews.

93c5de71a522be092dc7bcd05ed912a1ebedd16d.tech: Razorpay built the Razorpay Oncall Agent, a multi-agent LLM system using LangGraph and RAG-based context retrieval. A Supervisor Agent creates an investigation strategy by querying two RAG systems (architecture/dependencies and alert-specific runbooks), then dispatches parallel specialist agents for Kubernetes pod health, Coralogix logs, PromQL performance metrics, and AWS infrastructure checks. Findings are stored as structured evidence with confidence scores, then correlated into an Incident Evidence Timeline to score hypotheses and select a root cause. It integrates with Slack and runs in shadow mode.
93c5de71a522be092dc7bcd05ed912a1ebedd16d.nontech: Razorpay, a finance infrastructure company, reduced the time on-call engineers spent investigating production incidents. Engineers previously spent 20–40 minutes manually connecting information across multiple monitoring systems. Razorpay created an AI “Oncall Agent” that runs parallel specialist checks and delivers a complete investigation for the engineer to review. After three months in shadow mode, it cut investigation time by 80% (to 90 seconds), improved resolution time by 50–60%, and saved 6–8 hours of engineering work weekly, with more consistent results across experience levels.

05456d7b068f2ebb4bbc32a147c7cad30d65324d.tech: Datadog built Bits AI SRE, an autonomous agent for production incident investigation in distributed systems. It runs an iterative hypothesis-testing loop: form hypotheses, validate via targeted queries against live telemetry, and repeat until a root cause. The approach shifts from summarizing many tool-call outputs to causal reasoning, using recursive decomposition into sub-hypotheses and pivoting when evidence fails.
05456d7b068f2ebb4bbc32a147c7cad30d65324d.nontech: Datadog created Bits AI SRE to help teams investigate and resolve production incidents in complex distributed systems. It follows an SRE-style process: it forms hypotheses, checks them against live telemetry, and digs deeper until it finds the root cause. Datadog reports up to a 95% reduction in time to resolution and emphasizes evaluation on real production incidents.

12e256e6ee4612aa25b67273d403e04a6b441694.tech: Block’s internal red team engagement “Operation Pale Fire” tested goose, an open-source AI coding agent that can take real-world actions via MCP extensions. The team demonstrated prompt injection through Google Calendar MCP invites using invisible zero-width Unicode characters, then pivoted to poisoning goose “shareable recipes” that append to the system prompt. Successful compromise combined social engineering with AI-specific vulnerabilities, leading to mitigations: calendar policy changes, zero-width stripping, recipe transparency, and prompt-injection detection merged into goose, plus monitoring/runbook updates.
12e256e6ee4612aa25b67273d403e04a6b441694.nontech: Block ran an internal security exercise, “Operation Pale Fire,” to find weaknesses in goose, its open-source AI coding agent used in production. The team showed how hidden messages in calendar invites and poisoned shared recipes could lead to a real compromise of an employee laptop, helped by social engineering. Block then improved calendar handling, recipe visibility, and prompt-injection detection, and updated monitoring and response steps.

2a6ab6d34188866e4af1090561fc96b237273a54.tech: PwC’s AIDA (AI-driven annotation) is a production AWS system for extracting structured insights from unstructured contracts. It combines rule-based extraction with LLM-powered natural-language query using Amazon Bedrock. A RAG pipeline uses Bedrock Embeddings Models, semantic indexing in OpenSearch Serverless, hybrid retrieval, and citation linking to source page text. Asynchronous processing runs OCR and extraction on ECS (AWS Fargate) coordinated via SQS, with results stored in Amazon RDS. Guardrails, human-in-the-loop review, CloudTrail auditability, and CloudWatch/X-Ray monitoring support regulated use.
2a6ab6d34188866e4af1090561fc96b237273a54.nontech: PwC built AIDA to help legal, compliance, and procurement teams pull structured information from long, unstructured contracts that usually require heavy manual review. The system uses rules plus AI question answering to process contracts at scale on AWS. Customer deployments report up to a 90% reduction in manual contract review time, including a 90% reduction in rights research time for a major film and TV studio, supporting faster retrieval and shorter review cycles.

2aadde64428e417f26c4b621ccf3fdbb5bf44cab.tech: Intercom doubled R&D throughput (merge PRs per head) over nine months by building an AI-first development platform centered on Claude Code. They avoided native plugin mechanisms by syncing plugins to disk via IT, used a base plugin with safety hooks/telemetry, layered developer tools and hundreds of specialized skills, enforced quality with automated hooks and LLM judges, and instrumented usage via Honeycomb plus anonymized Claude session JSON uploaded to S3.
2aadde64428e417f26c4b621ccf3fdbb5bf44cab.nontech: Intercom, a customer support SaaS company, boosted engineering output by treating internal AI adoption like a product. They built a large library of specialized AI “skills,” added strong quality checks, and tracked how people used the system. Over nine months, they doubled PR throughput, improved code quality, sped up time-to-market, and shifted culture toward agent-first work.

cade7cdd0a3e71c88d126cf2be54c7f2467a5b69.tech: Factory’s Signals analyzes AI agent sessions at scale using a multi-stage pipeline built around OpenAI’s batch API and GPT-5.2. Sessions are fetched from BigQuery, filtered to those with at least 30 agentic steps, then processed to extract structured “facets” and detect friction patterns. Findings are correlated with system logs and releases, stored in BigQuery and reported to Slack. When friction crosses predefined thresholds, Signals files Linear tickets that Droid picks up, implements fixes, and submits pull requests; human approval is required before merge.
cade7cdd0a3e71c88d126cf2be54c7f2467a5b69.nontech: Factory built Signals to understand how users experience its AI coding agent at scale, without exposing conversation content to human reviewers. It analyzes thousands of sessions to spot user friction and delight, then connects those patterns to system logs and releases. When friction crosses set thresholds, it automatically creates tickets and drives fixes through code changes, with human approval required before merging. Early results report 73% of issues auto-resolved, with average fix time under 4 hours.

193f8d8e77cd9c2d83141466a8a8fedf0c39adf4.tech: OpenAI’s Frontier Product Exploration team ran a five-month experiment building an internal Electron app with zero human-written code, generating over one million lines across 1,500+ pull requests. They developed “harness engineering” principles and Symphony, an Elixir-based multi-agent orchestration system with six layers (policy, configuration, coordination, execution, integration, observability) and CLI-first control. Agents handled the full PR lifecycle, including autonomous review, merge conflict resolution, and CI/CD merging to main, with observability via Vector, VictoriaMetrics, Grafana, and distributed tracing.
193f8d8e77cd9c2d83141466a8a8fedf0c39adf4.nontech: OpenAI’s Frontier Product Exploration team tested whether software can be built and maintained by AI agents without humans writing the code. Over five months, they produced a large internal app through thousands of pull requests, using infrastructure and guardrails so agents could review, resolve conflicts, and deploy. The outcome was high throughput and a shift in human work toward building the systems that let agents operate autonomously.

82ce9ee2e13cc3f60ae35046a5892198823f44b3.tech: Hugging Face used OpenAI’s Codex coding agent to orchestrate an OCR-to-Markdown pipeline for “chat with paper.” They selected Chandra-OCR 2 via Hugging Face leaderboards (OlmOCRBench) and deployed it on Hugging Face Jobs serverless GPUs using vLLM. Processing ran across 16 parallel L40S instances, writing outputs to Hugging Face Buckets via hf-mount, then merging results for Paper Pages integration.
82ce9ee2e13cc3f60ae35046a5892198823f44b3.nontech: Hugging Face needed to convert about 27,000 academic papers into Markdown so users could “chat with paper” in HuggingChat. The papers lacked HTML versions on arXiv, so the team used OCR to fill the gap. They processed the full corpus in about 29–30 hours at an estimated cost of $850, enabling chat for all papers on the platform.

29af1cb43cb29569048c915187f1f4dc0c1bdd89.tech: DoorDash used LLM-generated merchant and item profiles to standardize declarative content (ingredients, preparation, cuisine, dietary attributes, contextual info), then encoded those profiles with off-the-shelf embedding models. They orchestrated incremental embedding updates with Metaflow, refreshed embeddings only when underlying content changed, and evaluated models using an LLM-as-a-judge harness. Production retrieval used embedding-based retrieval with cosine similarity and temperature-controlled softmax, plus an item-level EBR pipeline with a fine-tuned Qwen 3 reranker consuming the query, top-k item profiles, and store profile.
29af1cb43cb29569048c915187f1f4dc0c1bdd89.nontech: DoorDash improved search and recommendations across food, grocery, retail, and gifting by creating richer, standardized descriptions of merchants and items using large language models, then turning those descriptions into embeddings for retrieval. They reduced null searches and improved conversion, and their generative personalized homepage carousels increased homepage order rate and offline precision. The approach also helped with cold-start and tail queries, including fairness for small merchants.

a997c2019332e4dec14a048ccae22e2f81f8c12c.tech: HumanLayer’s case study applies Dapr (CNCF graduated) to production AI agents via the Dapr Agents framework. It targets state loss during failures in long-running workflows, adding workflow orchestration with automatic failure detection/recovery, exactly-once execution guarantees, and support for 30+ state stores. It also covers pub/sub multi-agent collaboration, OpenTelemetry observability, and resiliency features like retry policies and circuit breakers.
a997c2019332e4dec14a048ccae22e2f81f8c12c.nontech: The case study explains why many AI agent systems struggle in real production use at scale—especially when failures happen mid-task and progress is lost, forcing expensive rework. It presents Dapr Agents as a way to make agent workflows more durable, automatically recover from failures, coordinate multiple agents, and provide full visibility for auditing and debugging.

6f6135e8311de79dc13c42cddfe0f98404c33352.tech: 1Password used AI agents to refactor and decompose a multi-million-line Go monolith (B5) for its Unified Access system. The toolchain combined Go SSA analysis, SQL parsing, and DataDog runtime coupling data via an MCP integration to produce domain ownership maps, coupling graphs, and prioritized extraction order. Agents automated a 3,000+ call site migration in hours using deterministic manifests, templates, and a playbook with failure modes and human escalation. They used git worktrees for parallel multi-agent execution.
6f6135e8311de79dc13c42cddfe0f98404c33352.nontech: 1Password applied AI agents to refactor and break apart a large Go system that supports its Unified Access product. The team built tools that analyze code, database dependencies, and production behavior to plan safe service extraction. Agents handled a large migration of 3,000+ call sites quickly, improving productivity by about 20–30% on complex tasks, but they needed human oversight for sequencing and system boundaries.

5a493d7716cd650ebfdc403000bbba8286c13d29.tech: LinkedIn built an agent-based framework to accelerate model experimentation and infrastructure development by using LLMs to optimize the AI development process itself. The system combines code-authoring agents for distributed training, comprehensive evaluation for correctness and quality, and GPU microscheduling for efficient compute utilization. “Autopilot for Torch” runs iterative generate–verify–refine loops with structured verifier feedback, then validates on development GPU pods and promotes via Flyte workflows. Early results report strong performance across 100+ OpenML benchmarks, offline metric parity for internal workloads, and 10%+ training throughput improvements on optimized LLM workloads.
5a493d7716cd650ebfdc403000bbba8286c13d29.nontech: LinkedIn created an internal AI system that helps teams build better AI faster by improving the development process itself. It uses agents to generate code, check it against clear quality requirements, and refine it repeatedly. The approach supports large-scale work like migrating TensorFlow models to PyTorch, with validation and deployment through existing production workflows. Early results include strong benchmark performance, matching internal offline results, and higher training throughput, alongside reduced manual effort.

8502b35f72b4c4d661e515bfcc258244f11b5ff6.tech: Uber’s design systems team built uSpec, an agentic system that connects AI agents in Cursor to Figma via the open-source Figma Console MCP (Model Context Protocol). Agents use modular skills to generate component specs (anatomy, API docs, properties, color annotations, structure, and multi-platform accessibility) by reading Figma data and rendering templates back into Figma locally.
8502b35f72b4c4d661e515bfcc258244f11b5ff6.nontech: Uber’s design systems team needed accurate, complete documentation for hundreds of components across multiple technology stacks. They built uSpec to automatically generate full component specifications from Figma, including accessibility details. The system runs locally for enterprise security, produces specs in minutes instead of weeks, and improves consistency and accuracy across the design system.

09b0126b6ebf17a437a544be100823df10dbcd5c.tech: Software Factory builds a Notion-like collaborative editor (“Memo”) via a fully automated SDLC run by AI agents on the Owner orchestration platform. About 14 scheduled automations maintain the codebase, using GitHub Issues/CLI as the state engine and Sentry for monitoring-driven incident response and bug fixing. Metrics report 88% of PRs completed autonomously, with CI pass ratios around 98%.
09b0126b6ebf17a437a544be100823df10dbcd5c.nontech: Software Factory ran a two-week public experiment to see whether AI agents can handle the full software development lifecycle for a Notion-like collaborative app called Memo. Agents planned, built, reviewed, and deployed the product, with 88% of pull requests finished without human intervention. The project also used monitoring to detect problems and feed fixes back into development.

bb84d11c44a5374648dd227854e84f6f11dd4dc0.tech: Block built “Builderbot,” an agentic code review system positioned as a continuous “protector” rather than a passive assistant. It shifts protection left by running local checks before push using a standardized Just CLI contract. Reviews use progressive context disclosure via AGENTS.md, Amp’s Code Review Checks in .agents/checks/*.md, and Agent Skills for dynamic context loading.
bb84d11c44a5374648dd227854e84f6f11dd4dc0.nontech: Block, a finance company, faced resilience problems as teams shipped changes that looked fine locally but harmed the overall architecture. It created “Builderbot,” an agentic code review system that acts as a vigilant guardian. The approach catches issues pre-push, reduces the burden on human reviewers, and helps keep changes aligned with the organization’s architectural model.

c42e8a3659e746e6bd01f8e95d5185c0792ec846.tech: Uber built an internal AI agent infrastructure for software development at scale, using a layered stack on top of Michelangelo. It includes a model gateway, an internal context layer (source code, docs, Slack, JIRA), an MCP Gateway for unified agent/data integration, and tools like Minion, Uber Agent Builder, and AIFX CLI. Specialized agents include uReview, Autocover, and Shepherd. Adoption metrics include 84% agentic coding users, 65–72% AI-generated IDE code, and 11% PRs opened by agents, alongside a 6x AI cost increase since 2024 and token cost optimization efforts.
c42e8a3659e746e6bd01f8e95d5185c0792ec846.nontech: Uber created internal infrastructure so software engineers could use AI agents for development tasks, aiming to reduce “boring” work like upgrades, migrations, and trivial bug fixes and free engineers for more creative work. The company built tools for agent deployment, cost control, and workflow changes, including background agents, a no-code agent builder, and specialized agents for code review, test generation, and migrations. Results showed broad adoption (84% agentic coding users; 92% monthly agent use) but also higher AI costs (6x since 2024) and slower-than-expected uptake that required cultural change and peer-driven sharing.

9bcc46ce1e7003a141aaa22f06091378e23aa282.tech: Cursor reports using Cursor Automations to run a fleet of autonomous security agents for continuous vulnerability detection and remediation. Four templates are described: Agentic Security Review, Vuln Hunter, Anybump, and Invariant Sentinel. Agents use a custom security MCP tool deployed as a serverless Lambda function for persistent storage, deduplication, and consistent Slack-formatted reporting.
9bcc46ce1e7003a141aaa22f06091378e23aa282.nontech: Cursor says its PR volume grew 5x over nine months, making traditional security approaches insufficient at scale. To address this, it built Cursor Automations that continuously find and repair vulnerabilities. The company describes four templates for reviewing new PRs, scanning existing code, updating dependencies, and monitoring compliance daily, with results shared through Slack.

e0056791291fc9080b60a7afc7cb46b9289f269d.tech: Memo is built on the Ona platform using AI agents and automation loops to run a largely autonomous software factory. The pipeline includes hourly feature planning and feature builder PR generation, PR reviewer conversation threads for iterative fixes, and an hourly PR shepherd fail-safe that rebases, resolves merge conflicts, and addresses build failures. Post-merge verification deploys and smoke tests, creating high-priority bug reports on failures. It also integrates Sentry with an incident responder automation every 15 minutes, plus daily metrics updates for lines of code, PRs merged, and test coverage.
e0056791291fc9080b60a7afc7cb46b9289f269d.nontech: The case study describes how a team built Memo, a note-taking application, using AI agents and automation loops on the Ona platform. The system manages the full development lifecycle—from planning features to deploying and checking results—so human involvement is minimal. It processes pull requests, fixes bugs, and improves its own workflows, aiming to increase development speed while catching production issues through automated verification and reporting.

696ef433e2652d1f70a03a260c4693231edde36b.tech: Atlassian built Fireworks, a Firecracker microVM orchestration platform on Kubernetes, in four weeks using Rovo Dev AI agents with minimal human-written code. The platform includes a scheduler, autoscaler, node agents, envoy ingress layers, raft persistence, and features like 100ms warm starts, live migration, eBPF network policy enforcement, shared volumes, snapshot restore, and sidecar sandboxes.
696ef433e2652d1f70a03a260c4693231edde36b.nontech: Atlassian used its Rovo Dev AI agent system to build a production-ready platform for running Firecracker microVMs on Kubernetes in four weeks. The goal was to create a secure execution engine with advanced capabilities like fast startup, live migration, and network controls. They did this by letting AI agents handle development, testing, and CI/CD workflows with strong automated validation.

afeab4f057719a1fe7c51754d0c0f03a0e096a33.tech: Cloudflare built a CI-native, multi-agent AI code review orchestration around OpenCode. A coordinator spawns OpenCode via Bun.spawn, streams JSONL events, and launches up to seven specialized reviewers (security, performance, code quality, documentation, release, compliance) through a runtime plugin. Findings are produced as structured XML, deduplicated and filtered by a judge pass, with risk-tiered model selection, prompt/token optimization, and circuit-breaker failback.
afeab4f057719a1fe7c51754d0c0f03a0e096a33.nontech: Cloudflare created a production AI code review system to reduce delays from manual reviews, where first-review wait times were measured in hours. Instead of generic tools, it uses multiple focused AI reviewers working in parallel, coordinated to produce review comments quickly. In the first month it processed many merge requests across thousands of repositories, with a median review time of minutes and a low manual override rate.

282f9086b1b860671e7424fef4302dff140a4634.tech: LinkedIn built three agentic workflows for Liger Kernel GPU kernel engineering: liger-kernel-dev (PyTorch→Triton kernel creation), liger-autopatch (HuggingFace Transformers model integration), and liger-kernel-perf (performance optimization). Each uses a three-stage pipeline—understanding, acting, verification—with structured profiles and human review checkpoints. The perf loop profiles kernels, optionally uses NVIDIA NCU, generates versioned variants, and blocks regressions beyond a 5% guardrail.
282f9086b1b860671e7424fef4302dff140a4634.nontech: LinkedIn used AI agents to speed up and scale GPU kernel work for its open-source Liger Kernel project. Instead of experts manually writing and optimizing custom Triton kernels, the agents automate kernel creation, model integration, and performance tuning through a step-by-step process with human checks. The result included faster kernels, model updates that needed only review, and major internal training efficiency gains.
